title: Fitting flexible hoses to a basin tap
subtitle: Connect a basin tap to the supply pipes using braided stainless hoses
excerpt: How to fit braided stainless-steel flexible hoses between a basin tap and the supply pipes: reaching the back-nut, connecting the hose ends, and checking for drips after restoring the supply.

glossaryTerms.definition: A braided stainless-steel supply hose with fittings at each end for connecting a tap to the supply pipe. One end has a swivel nut that engages the tap tail; the other has a compression or push-fit fitting for the supply pipe. Most modern flexible hoses include an integral quarter-turn isolation valve on the supply end.

The large plastic or brass nut on the underside of a basin tap that draws the tap down against the basin when tightened. Accessible only from beneath the basin, and typically unreachable with a standard spanner — the basin wrench is the tool for this nut.

content.text: Modern basin taps come with or are designed for
flexible tap hoses

in place of rigid pipe connections. Flexible hoses are faster to fit, tolerate minor misalignment between the tap tail and the supply pipe, and include a built-in isolation valve so the tap can be serviced without shutting off the whole supply. The only awkward part of the job is tightening the
tap back-nut
in the confined space under the basin.
Step-by-step
Step 1, isolate the supply.
Turn off the service valves on the existing supply pipes (or the main stopcock if there are no service valves). Open the tap to drain any residual pressure.
Step 2, fit the tap to the basin.

Drop the tap into the basin hole with a plastic or rubber top hat washer underneath the tap body to protect the basin. Thread the tap tail through the hole from above. Fit the back washer and back-nut from below, and tighten the back-nut using the basin wrench until the tap is firm and correctly aligned. Do not use a power tool: the plastic nut on ceramic basins cracks under high torque.
Step 3, connect the hose to the tap tail.

Thread the swivel nut on the hose top end onto the tap tail. Apply two wraps of PTFE tape to the tap tail thread first if the fitting is a metal-to-metal connection. Tighten the swivel nut by hand until snug, then a quarter turn with the adjustable spanner.
Step 4, connect the hose to the supply pipe.

Fit the lower end of the hose to the supply pipe using the compression or push-fit fitting supplied. Ensure the hose runs without kinking or sharp bends; a kinked hose restricts flow and will fail at the kink. Close the integral isolation valve, then restore the main supply.
Step 5, test.

Open the integral isolation valve on each hose. Run hot and cold water and check both connections for drips at the tap tail, at the supply pipe fitting, and at the isolation valve body. Dry the hoses and fittings and recheck after five minutes.
Common mistakes
Cross-threading the swivel nut.
The swivel nut must start straight on the tap tail. Start by hand; if resistance is felt immediately, back off and re-align before continuing.
Mixing hot and cold hoses.
Mark the hot and cold hoses before removing the old tap. Hot on the left, cold on the right is standard in the UK, but check the existing positions rather than assuming.
